coredata.dc:title: Antibacterial and osteogenic carbon quantum dots for regeneration of bone defects infected with multidrug-resistant bacteria
coredata.dc:creator: Geng, Bijiang
Li, Ping
Fang, Fuling
Shi, Wenyan
Glowacki, Julie
Pan, Dengyu
Shen, Longxiang
coredata.dc:description: Intractable bacterial infections and insufficient osseointegration are two major concerns plaguing the repair of infected bone defects. Many attempts use antibiotics coatings on the implant surface to prevent infectious biofilm formation and promote bone regeneration, however, the introduction of toxic antibiotics not only leads to the appearance of multidrug-resistant (MDR) bacteria, but also decreases osteoinductivity of scaffold materials. Herein, we report for the first time the systemic regulation of the antibacterial and osteogenic activities of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) by changing their surface charge. The positively charged CQDs (p-CQDs) exhibited excellent antibacterial activity against MDR bacteria and suppression of biofilm formation, whereas negatively charged CQDs (n-CQDs) could significantly promoted bone regeneration. To avoid aggregation of p-CQDs and n-CQDs in scaffolds, we first fabricated nearly neutral p-CQD/WS2 hybrids by depositing p-CQDs on WS2 nanosheets (NSs). We then constructed a multifunctional biodegradable p-CQD/WS2 and n-CQDs co-encapsulated GelMA hydrogel scaffolds. In a MRSA-infected craniotomy defect model, implantation of multifunctional p-CQD/WS2/n-CQD/GelMA hydrogel scaffolds resulted in nearly complete repair of infected bone defect with the new bone area of 97.0 ± 1.6% at 60 days. These findings indicate a surface charge regulation strategy for designing biomaterials with both antibacterial and osteogenic activities for treating infected bone defects.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation and characterization of p-CQDs. (a) Schematic of the synthetic processes of p-CQDs. (b) Absorption and fluorescence spectra of p-CQDs. (c–d) TEM and HRTEM images of p-CQDs. (e–f) XRD pattern and FTIR spectrum of p-CQDs. (g) Survey XPS, high-resolution C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s spectra of p-CQDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Antibacterial activity of p-CQDs. (a) Typical colony formation of p-CQD treated E. coli, S. aureus, and MRSA on LB agar plates at the varied concentration (0–9 μg/mL). (b) SEM images of E. coli, S. aureus, and MRSA before treatment and after treatment with p-CQDs. (c) The live/dead staining images of E. coli, S. aureus, and MRSA after treatment with p-CQDs for different times (0–60 min). (d) Typical photographs of skin wounds in MRSA-infected mice treated with PBS solution (control), commercial woundplast, or p-CQDs for 0, 2, 4, 6, 9, and 12 days. (e) Photographs of LB-agar plate images of surviving bacteria. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Preparation and characterization of p-CQD/WS2/n-CQD/GelMA hydrogels. (a) Schematic illustration of the preparation of p-CQD/WS2/n-CQD/GelMA hydrogels. (b) DLS spectrum of WS2 nanosheets and p-CQD/WS2 hybrids. (c) Zeta potential of WS2 nanosheets, p-CQDs, and p-CQD/WS2 hybrids. (d) UV–vis absorption spectra of WS2 nanosheets, p-CQDs, and p-CQD/WS2 hybrids. (e) Survey XPS spectrum, high-resolution W 4f, S 2p, C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s spectra of p-CQD/WS2 hybrids. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Antibacterial activity of p-CQD/WS2/n-CQD/GelMA hydrogels. (a) Images of GelMA, n-CQD/GelMA, p-CQD/WS2/GelMA, and p-CQD/WS2/n-CQD/GelMA hydrogels before and after UV light irradiation. (b) SEM images of GelMA, n-CQD/GelMA, p-CQD/WS2/GelMA, and p-CQD/WS2/n-CQD/GelMA hydrogels. (c–d) Swelling ratio and compression modulus evaluation of GelMA, n-CQD/GelMA, p-CQD/WS2/GelMA, and p-CQD/WS2/n-CQD/GelMA hydrogels. (e) Typical colony formation of the hydrogel-treated E. coli, S. aureus, and MRSA on LB agar plates. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Osteogenic activity of p-CQD/WS2/n-CQD/GelMA hydrogels. (a–c) Alizarin red S stained images, ALP activity, and quantification of the stained mineral deposits of hMSCs cultured on GelMA, n-CQD/GelMA, p-CQD/WS2/GelMA, and p-CQD/WS2/n-CQD/GelMA hydrogels. (d) The percentages of new bone areas in calvarial defects. (e) Micro-CT images of calvarial defects treated with GelMA, n-CQD/GelMA, p-CQD/WS2/GelMA, and p-CQD/WS2/n-CQD/GelMA hydrogels for 0, 30, or 60 days. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Antibacterial and osteogenic carbon quantum dots for regeneration of bone defects infected with multidrug-resistant bacteria

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Intractable bacterial infections and insufficient osseointegration are two major concerns plaguing the repair of infected bone defects. Many attempts use antibiotics coatings on the implant surface to prevent infectious biofilm formation and promote bone regeneration, however, the introduction of toxic antibiotics not only leads to the appearance of multidrug-resistant (MDR) bacteria, but also decreases osteoinductivity of scaffold materials. Herein, we report for the first time the systemic regulation of the antibacterial and osteogenic activities of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) by changing their surface charge. The positively charged CQDs (p-CQDs) exhibited excellent antibacterial activity against MDR bacteria and suppression of biofilm formation, whereas negatively charged CQDs (n-CQDs) could significantly promoted bone regeneration. To avoid aggregation of p-CQDs and n-CQDs in scaffolds, we first fabricated nearly neutral p-CQD/WS2 hybrids by depositing p-CQDs on WS2 nanosheets (NSs). We then constructed a multifunctional biodegradable p-CQD/WS2 and n-CQDs co-encapsulated GelMA hydrogel scaffolds. In a MRSA-infected craniotomy defect model, implantation of multifunctional p-CQD/WS2/n-CQD/GelMA hydrogel scaffolds resulted in nearly complete repair of infected bone defect with the new bone area of 97.0 ± 1.6% at 60 days. These findings indicate a surface charge regulation strategy for designing biomaterials with both antibacterial and osteogenic activities for treating infected bone defects.

Overall scheme to show the main synthesis procedures of p-CQD/WS2/n-CQD/GelMA hydrogel scaffolds and their applications for regeneration of bone defects infected with multidrug-resistant bacteria.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Bone defect-related infections, caused by bacterial adhesion and biofilm formation on the implant surface, are considered as an intractable public-health problem worldwide [1–3]. In clinical therapies, traditional antibiotic treatments are often unsatisfactory for eradicating the formed bacterial biofilm, thus requiring repeated surgery and costly implant removal [4–7]. More seriously, the overuse of antibiotics leads to the wide-spreading of multidrug-resistant (MDR) bacteria, such as the methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA) [8,9]. Furthermore, toxicity, anaphylaxis, and other side effects of small-molecule antibiotics substantially restrict their clinical application [10]. Hence, it is urgent to develop biocompatible nanomaterials with broad-spectrum antibacterial properties to prevent the formation of biofilm.

Insufficient osseointegration is another problem plaguing infected bone defects. Although various materials have been reported to achieve excellent antibacterial ability or osteogenic activity individually [11–13], but the artificial implant materials with single function are not enough for repairing infected bone defects. Many attempts have been made to modify metallic implants with bioactive moieties including growth factors, proteins, and peptides for promoting osteogenic differentiation and accelerating bone regeneration [13,14]. Meanwhile, some studies attempted to use the antibacterial components (such as Ag nanoparticles (NPs), antibiotics, etc.) as surface coatings for the purpose of preventing biofilm formation [15–17]. For example, nanosilica and Ag NPs co-modified hydrogels were fabricated to enhance osteogenesis differentiation in infected bone defects [18]. However, good antibacterial ability and biosafety of artificial implants are often contradictory since the antibacterial ability is often accompanied by severe cytotoxicity. The long-term in vivo toxicity induced by the uncontrolled release of heavy metal ions may inhibits bone regeneration [19,20]. Furthermore, similar to antibiotics, the lone-term use of antimicrobial Ag NPs produces corresponding multidrug-resistant bacteria [21], hindering their practical applications in preventing the formation of biofilms. Alternatively, antibacterial photothermal therapy (PTT) and photodynamic therapy (PDT) have been applied in eradicating MRSA biofilm [8,11,22–24], but most PTT or PDT agents cannot promote cell proliferation and osteogenesis differentiation after NIR laser irradiation. In view of this, it is necessary to develop multifunctional biomaterials that simultaneously possess excellent antibacterial ability and osteogenic activity for effectively killing MRSA and promoting bone tissue regeneration.

Recently, carbon-based nanomaterials including graphene, carbon nanotubes (CNTs), fullerene, and carbon or graphene quantum dots (CQDs or GQDs) have been reported to possess antibacterial activity as well as low cytotoxicity toward mammalian cells [25–30]. As an emerging carbonaceous nanomaterials, zero-dimensional (0D) CQDs with outstanding fluorescent properties, excellent biocompatibility, and ease of surface modification are widely used in many fields including bioimaging [31–33], biosensing [34,35], gene/drug delivery [36,37], cancer therapy [38–42], catalysts [43,44], and optoelectronic devices [45–47]. Recently, although the biomedicine application of CQDs has been extended to antibacterial therapy [48–50] and tissue engineering [28,51], dual-purpose scaffold materials based on antibacterial and osteogenic CQDs have not been reported. This may be ascribed to the unclear relationship between the CQDs’ structure and their antibacterial or osteogenic activities. We tested the hypothesis that the surface structure characteristics of CQDs can be designed to achieve a rational regulation of their antibacterial ability and osteogenic activities.

In this work, we report a novel, simple microwave-assisting molecular fusion method to produce antibacterial or osteogenic CQDs by regulating their surface charge. Positively charged amine-functionalized CQDs (p-CQDs, 39.4 ± 2.0 mV) with excellent antibacterial activity were synthesized from spermidine. The obtained p-CQDs exhibited broad-spectrum antibacterial activity not only against nonmulti drug-resistant (non-MDR) bacteria (E. coli and S. aureus), but also against MDR bacteria (MRSA). The high biocompatibility of p-CQDs was then confirmed by in vitro cytotoxicity, oxidative stress analyses, and histopathological analysis, without showing any negative effect on osteogenesis differentiation. We further synthesize the negatively charged sulfo-functionalized CQDs (n-CQDs, −15.2 ± 1.2 mV) with good osteogenic activity using 1,3,6-trinitropyrene and sodium sulfite as the precursor following our previously reported [28]. For preventing possible agglomeration caused by electrostatic interaction between positively charged p-CQDs and negatively charged n-CQDs, the p-CQDs were first deposited on the surface of WS2 nanosheets (NSs) to form the nearly neutral p-CQD/WS2 hybrids. As a 2D nanomaterial, the recently developed WS2 NSs possess excellent biocompatibility and stable physicochemical properties, bringing a wide range of promising applications in drug delivery [52], tumor imaging [29], biosensing [53], and cancer therapy [54]. More importantly, WS2-based nanomaterials could be served as the excellent reinforcing agents to enhance the mechanical property of biodegradable hydrogels [55]. After depositing, the controlled release of p-CQDs can also be achieved, which was beneficial for subsequent repair of infected bone defect. Finally, we constructed a multifunctional 3D p-CQD/WS2/n-CQD/GelMA hydrogel scaffold, which contains p-CQD/WS2 hybrids to kill MDR bacterial and n-CQDs to enhance osteogenic differentiation. In vitro and in vivo results demonstrate that p-CQD/WS2 and n-CQD co-incorporated GelMA hydrogels possess efficient broad-spectrum antibacterial ability and excellent osteogenic activity even in the presence of MRSA, achieving nearly complete repair of infected bone defect with the new bone area of 97.0 ± 1.6% in 60 days.

The ultrafast microwave-assisted method was utilized to synthesize positively charged CQDs (p-CQDs) and negatively charged CQDs (n-CQDs). For p-CQDs, 0.1 g spermidine trihydrochloride was added to 10 mL DI water and the microwave reaction was carried out at 200 °C for 3 min. For n-CQDs, we first synthesized 1,3,6-trinitropyrene (TNP) according to our previous report [39]. Thereafter, 0.1 g TNP and 0.25 g Na2SO3 were added to 10 mL DI water and the microwave reaction was carried out at 200 °C for 3 min. After a simple microwave reaction, the obtained CQD solutions were dialyzed for three days to remove unreacted precursors.

We utilized a ball grinder (MITR-YXQM-2L) to grind the commercial tungsten disulfide (WS2) powder for 4 h and to disperse 30 mg WS2 powder in 30 mL H2SO4. After heating at 90 °C for 24 h, WS2 nanosheets (NSs) were obtained through centrifugation (10000 rpm, 10 min) and washed for three times to remove the residual H2SO4. Single-layer WS2 NSs were obtained after the sonication for 2 h.

The p-CQDs (0.4 mg/mL) were deposited on the surface of WS2 NSs (0.2 mg/mL) by vigorous stirring for 24 h. The resulting p-CQD/WS2 hybrids were collected by centrifugation and washed for three times to remove the un-bounded p-CQDs. The loading ratio of p-CQDs was determined to be 100% after measuring the changed mass of WS2 NSs before and after p-CQDs deposition.

Gelatin/Methacrylate Anhydride (GelMA) was synthesized according to previous report [56] using Type A porcine skin gelatin and methacrylic anhydride as the precursors. The p-CQD/WS2/n-CQD/GelMA hydrogels were then constructed through an UV light-activate photopolymerization of p-CQD/WS2 hybrids (0.4 mg/mL), n-CQDs (0.4 mg/mL), and 15% (w/v) GelMA in the presence of Irgacure 2959 (0.01 g) photoinitiator. The UV light irradiation time was 1 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of p-CQDs, n-CQDs, and p-CQD/WS2 hybrids
ce:section.ce:para: TEM images of p-CQDs, n-CQDs, and p-CQD/WS2 hybrids were evaluated by JEM-2100F microscope. XRD patterns, FT-IR spectra, and XPS characterization of p-CQDs, n-CQDs, and p-CQD/WS2 hybrids were investigated by D/max-2550, Nicolet AVATAR 370 FT-IR, and Kratos Axis Ultra DLD spectrometer, respectively. The hydrodynamic diameter and zeta potential of these samples were determined by the Zetasizer Nano ZS90. The optical properties of p-CQDs and n-CQDs were detected by a Hitachi 3100 and 7000 spectrophotometers, respectively.

SEM images of p-CQD/WS2/n-CQD/GelMA hydrogels were recorded by S-4800 microscope (Hitachi, Japan). For the degradation rate measurement, the p-CQD/WS2/n-CQD/GelMA hydrogels were immersed in PBS, DI water, or DMEM for 600 h. The hydrogels were washed for three times at the different time points and masses were weighed. An Instron 5944 system was employed to assess the mechanical properties of p-CQD/WS2/n-CQD/GelMA hydrogels following the previous report [28].

hMSCs (Cyagen Biosciences, Suzhou, China) were used to assess the biocompatibility of p-CQDs, n-CQDs, and p-CQD/WS2 hybrids. Briefly, hMSCs were seeded in 96-well plates and adhered overnight. Medium was replaced with fresh medium containing p-CQDs, n-CQDs, or p-CQD/WS2 hybrids. 20 μL of MTT reagent was added and incubated. Finally, 150 μL DMSO was added to replace the medium and the 490 nm absorbance was measured to determine the cell viabilities.

The E. coli (ATCC 25922), S. aureus (ATCC 8095), and MRSA (ATCC 43300) bacteria were grown separately in the Luria-Bertani (LB) media and harvested until the absorbance at 600 nm (OD600) of bacteria reached 1.0. Prior to further use, the bacteria were centrifuged and washed for three times with PBS (pH 7.4). For the measurement of MICs, we utilized a standard colony counting method to determine the MICs of p-CQDs against different bacterial. After incubating with p-CQDs for 3 h, the bacterial were dispersed in 5 mM PBS with shaking (250 rpm) for another 1 h at 37 °C. Next, the bacteria solutions were diluted 100 times and 100 μL of suspension was spread on the LB agar plates. The lowest concentration of p-CQDs capable of completely inhibiting bacterial growth was determined to be the MIC.

After p-CQD treatment, E. coli, S. aureus, and MRSA bacteria were incubated with PI and SYTO 9 for 30 min. The suspension (5 μL) was pipetted onto a microscope slide and covered with a coverslip. We utilized the confocal microscope to evaluate the bacterial.

After p-CQD treatment, E. coli, S. aureus, and MRSA bacteria were deposited on silicon wafers and fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde at 4 °C for 12 h. The bacteria were then dehydrated by a series of 30, 50, 70, 80, 90, and 100% of ethanol. SEM images of bacteria were then recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibacterial activity and wound healing test in vivo
ce:section.ce:para: All animal experiments were approved by the Animal Care and Experiment Committee of Shanghai Sixth People's Hospital affiliated with Shanghai Jiao Tong University (2020-KY-036(K)). Five-week old Balb/c mice were bought from the SLAC. (Shanghai, China). Three groups were divided as following: control, p-CQDs, and commercial band-aid woundplast. After anesthesia, 100 μL MRSA (1 × 108 CFU/mL) was applied to the wounds (diameter = 1 cm) to construct the infection model. 20 μL samples were then added to the wounds and the final concentration of p-CQDs was 200 μg/mL. The wound tissue was extracted after 2 or 12 days and placed in sterile saline. After incubation for 12 h, we utilized the spread-plate method to measure the numbers of bacteria in solution. The mice were photographed at 2, 6, and 12 days and sacrificed to extract the wound tissues for H&E staining and Masson staining.

We first performed the live/dead cell staining assay to evaluate the biocompatibility of p-CQD/WS2/n-CQD/GelMA hydrogels. Briefly, hMSCs were seeded on p-CQD/WS2/n-CQD/GelMA hydrogels. After the incubation of 48 h, the biocompatibility of hydrogels was assessed by the kit. The osteogenic differentiation activity was then investigated by ALP activity assay and ARS staining. hMSCs were seeded on these hydrogels and incubated for 7 or 14 days. After incubation, hMSCs were collected by centrifugation and the ALP activity of these samples were evaluated by ALP assay kit (Beyotime, China). For ARS staining, after incubation, 2% alizarin red was utilized to stain the hMSCs. The fluorescence microscope was employed to record the images.

Five-week old Balb/c mice were divided into four groups: control, n-CQD/GelMA, p-CQD/WS2/GelMA hydrogels, and p-CQD/WS2/n-CQD/GelMA hydrogels. After anesthesia, the mice were operated to create an infected craniotomy defect model (diameter = 6 mm) with the addition of 100 μL MRSA (1 × 108 CFU/mL). Then, 100 μL n-CQD/GelMA, p-CQD/WS2/GelMA, or p-CQD/WS2/n-CQD/GelMA were injected into the infected defect sites. After UV light irradiation for 1 min, the n-CQD/GelMA hydrogels, p-CQD/WS2/GelMA hydrogels, and p-CQD/WS2/n-CQD/GelMA hydrogels were formed, respectively. At day 0, 30, and 60, we performed Micro CT to evaluate the osteogenesis efficiency of these hydrogels. Additionally, the harvested samples were decalcified and embedded in paraffin for histological analysis.

All data are presented as mean ± standard deviation (n = 5). Statistical differences were analyzed using a Student's t-test. ∗ denotes a statistical significance (∗p < 0.05, ∗∗p < 0.01 and ∗∗∗p < 0.001) between the experimental data of two groups.

The antibacterial p-CQDs with highly positive charge were synthesized through a one-step microwave reaction at 200 °C for 3 min using spermidine trihydrochloride as the precursors (Fig. 1 a). After centrifugation and dialysis, the purified p-CQDs exhibited cyan fluorescence upon 365 nm UV light excitation (Fig. 1b) and a high quantum yield of 34.3% (Fig. S1). The excitation-dependent PL behavior of p-CQDs was also observed in Fig. S2a, which could be attributed to the formation of different sizes of p-CQDs. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of p-CQDs presented in Fig. 1b exhibited a characteristic peak around 260 nm. After continuous UV exposure for 5 h, the PL intensity of p-CQD solutions exhibited insignificant reduction (Fig. S2b), verifying the excellent fluorescence stability. Besides, the PL intensity of p-CQD solutions in different pH value (2–10) revealed negligible change (Fig. S2c), further confirming the outstanding photostability of antibacterial p-CQDs. The Zeta potential of p-CQDs at the concentration of 100 μg/mL was determined to be 39.4 ± 2.0 mV (Fig. S3a), indicating that the antibacterial p-CQDs possessed super-cationic feature. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image showed that p-CQDs were nearly monodispersed with average particle sizes of 4.7 ± 1.1 nm (Fig. 1c and S4a). Meanwhile, the high-resolution TEM image illustrated that the crystal spacing of p-CQDs was 0.21 nm (Fig. 1d and S4b), which can be attributed to the (100) diffraction planes of graphite [57]. The graphite-like structure of p-CQDs was further verified by the X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern (Fig. 1e), revealing a broad diffraction peak at around 24° corresponded to the (002) layer spacing. In the Raman spectroscopy (Fig. S3b), the intensity of disordered D to ordered G band (ID/IG) of p-CQDs was measured to be 1.10, indicating that a number of defects could be presented in the edge of p-CQDs owing to a large amount of functional groups.

We then carried out X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy to unveil the chemical composition and structure of p-CQDs. The survey XPS spectra of p-CQDs can be found in Fig. 1g, revealing the presence of C (75.0 at.%), N (14.7 at.%), and O species (10.3 at.%). Three different types of C–C/CC, C–OH, and C–N were observed in the high-resolution C 1s spectrum of p-CQDs. In the high-resolution N 1s spectrum, two peaks at 398.8 and 400.3 eV were assigned to –NH2 and graphitic N, respectively, suggesting that the super-cationic feature of p-CQDs could be ascribed to the amine functionalization. Furthermore, the high-resolution O 1s spectrum revealed the presence of hydroxyl (O–H) in the edge of p-CQDs. Apart from XPS spectra, the existence of –NH2 and –OH was further verified by the FTIR spectra (Fig. 1f).

We first utilized a standard colony counting method to evaluate the minimal inhibitory concentration (MIC) values of p-CQDs in two non-MDR bacterial strains (E. coli and S. aureus) and one strain of MDR bacteria (MRSA). The p-CQDs exhibited excellent inhibitory activities against the three bacterial strains with an increased concentration from 0 to 9 μg/mL (Fig. 2 a). For Gram-negative bacteria (E. coli), no colony on the LB agar plates was observed when the concentration of p-CQDs was 6 μg/mL. Notably, the MIC values of p-CQDs for non-MDR and MDR bacterial strain both were 3 μg/mL. The live/dead staining assay was further performed to evaluate the antibacterial activity of p-CQDs. Fluorescent imaging showed that more red spots were observed in the three bacterial strains after the treatment of p-CQDs for 60 min (Fig. 2c), indicating that p-CQDs possessed broad-spectrum antibacterial ability. The anti-biofilm activity of p-CQDs was also evaluated to assess antibacterial property. The relative biofilm biomasses in the p-CQD group (9 μg/mL) were calculated to be 9.5 ± 4.0%, 4.8 ± 2.1%, and 5.0 ± 1.2% for E. coli, S. aureus, and MRSA, respectively (Fig. S5 and S6), which was much lower than those in the control group. These results suggested that positively charged p-CQDs can be regarded as an antibacterial agent not only killing MDR bacteria but also preventing the formation of biofilm.

We next carried out the bacteria uptake and morphological observations to understand the antibacterial mechanism of p-CQDs. Considering that antibacterial p-CQDs possess super-cationic features, we proposed that the strong electrostatic and hydrophobic interactions between positively charged p-CQDs and negatively charged bacterial membrane could induce bacterial membrane damage. Utilizing the intrinsic fluorescence properties of p-CQDs, obvious aggregation of bacteria can be detected after the treatment of p-CQDs at the concentration of 20 μg/mL for 60 min (Fig. S7), which demonstrated our suspecting. The typical SEM images of E. coli, S. aureus, and MRSA with coherent cytomembrane structures were observed in Fig. 2b. However, the three bacterial strains treated with p-CQDs (20 μg/mL) for 2 h exhibited serious disruption of the bacterial membrane structures, further indicating that p-CQDs with highly positive charge could damage bacterial membrane and then induce the leakage of cellular contents. These results clearly illustrated that the antibacterial mechanism involved the damage of bacterial cell walls and membranes.

Having confirmed the potent antibacterial activity in vitro and clarified the antibacterial mechanism, we next assess the antibacterial efficacy of p-CQDs in vivo using the MRSA-infected skin wounds in mice as a model. Three groups (n = 5) were randomly divided: control, woundplast, and p-CQDs. Antibacterial efficacy was evaluated by measuring the wound area and surviving bacteria. Fig. 2d showed the wound photographs of mice in each groups at 0, 2, 4, 6, 9, and 12 days. Compared with the control and commercial woundplast groups, obvious reduction of wounds area was detected in the p-CQD group after 2 days. After 6 days, the wound area decreased below 20% in the p-CQD group (Fig. S8), suggesting a better recovery of the wound. Besides, we collected the wound tissues in each groups at 0 and 12 days. The number of colony forming units (CFU) in the p-CQD group at 12 days were much less than those in the control and commercial woundplast groups (Fig. 2e), demonstrating the great antibacterial property of p-CQDs against multidrug-resistant bacteria in vivo. Additionally, we performed the histological analysis to evaluate the antibacterial therapeutic effects of p-CQDs. After the treatment of 2, 6, or 12 days, the wounds of the mice in each groups were harvested for the H&E staining and Masson trichrome staining. For the H&E staining, less infected tissues (blue) can be observed in the p-CQD group compared with the control and commercial woundplast groups (Fig. S9). Besides, during the process of the wound healing, the formation of collagen fiber (blue) in the p-CQD group was also detected by the Masson trichrome staining (Fig. S10), indicating the success of skin regeneration. These results demonstrated that p-CQDs possessed excellent antibacterial activity in vivo, suggesting the great potential in treating multidrug-resistant bacterial infections.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of p-CQD/WS2/n-CQD/GelMA hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: In clinical therapy, bone-implant-related infections not only impede bone healing but also endanger the patient's health. In particular, MDR bacterial-induced biofilms would adhere on the surface of bone implants to cause osteomyelitis, often requiring a second operation to remove the implants. Thus, it is highly desirable to synthesize multifunctional biomaterials capable of killing bacteria and promoting bone regeneration simultaneously. Recently, the sulfo-functionalized n-CQDs were reported by our groups for enhancing osteogenic differentiation of hMSCs through BMP/Smad signaling pathway [28]. Inspired by this, a multifunctional 3D GelMA hydrogel scaffold was fabricated to incorporate p-CQDs with antibacterial activity and n-CQDs with osteocompatibility. To avoid the possible electrostatic interaction between positively charged p-CQDs and negatively charged n-CQDs, the p-CQDs were first deposited on the surface of WS2 NSs to form the nearly neutral p-CQD/WS2 hybrids. Then, we constructed a multifunctional 3D p-CQD/WS2/n-CQD/GelMA hydrogel scaffold, which contains p-CQD/WS2 hybrids to kill MDR bacterial and n-CQDs to enhance osteogenic differentiation.

We first investigated the structure characteristic and optical properties of n-CQDs including TEM, HRTEM, Raman, XRD, FTIR, absorption and fluorescence spectra (Fig. S11). TEM image illustrated that n-CQDs were nearly monodispersed with average particle sizes of 2.9 ± 1.2 nm (Fig. S11a). The crystal spacing of n-CQDs was determined to be 0.21 nm by the HRTEM image (Fig. S11b), which was attributed to the (100) diffraction planes of graphite. XRD pattern of n-CQDs presented in Fig. S11e revealed a broad diffraction peak at around 24° corresponded to the (002) layer spacing. In the Raman spectroscopy (Fig. S11d), the intensity of disordered D to ordered G band (ID/IG) of n-CQDs was measured to be 1.042, indicating that a number of defects could be presented in the edge of n-CQDs owing to a large amount of functional groups. Furthermore, the existence of –SO3 and –OH was verified by the FTIR spectra (Fig. S11f). The UV–vis absorption spectrum of n-CQDs exhibited a characteristic peak around 390 nm (Fig. S11c). For the fluorescence spectrum, the emission peak of n-CQDs located at approximately 480 nm under 380 nm excitation.

The rational design and synthetic process of p-CQD/WS2 hybrids were displayed in Fig. 3 a. The single-layer WS2 NSs were first synthesized from bulk WS2 powder through a liquid exfoliation method using H2SO4 as the intercalation agent. After intercalation and ultrasonication, the positively charged p-CQDs were deposited on the surface of negatively charged WS2 NSs through the electrostatic interaction. The TEM and HRTEM images of WS2 NSs were presented in Fig. S12a, b, revealing that the average size and interlayer spacing of WS2 NSs were measured to be 50–100 nm and 0.29 nm, respectively. For p-CQD/WS2 hybrids, p-CQDs with a size of 5 nm and an interlayer spacing of 0.21 nm were clearly observed in the TEM and HRTEM images (Fig. S12c, d), suggesting the successful loading of p-CQDs. Apart from TEM characterization, DLS results further revealed that the average hydrodynamic sizes of WS2 NSs before and after p-CQD depositing were both about 100 nm (Fig. 3b). The zeta potential of WS2 NSs, p-CQDs, and p-CQD/WS2 hybrids was measured to be −15.6 ± 1.0, 39.0 ± 1.4, and 5.9 ± 1.2 mV, respectively (Fig. 3c), illustrating the nearly neutral property of p-CQD/WS2 hybrids. Compared to WS2 NSs and p-CQDs, the enhanced optical absorption of p-CQD/WS2 hybrids at the same concentration can be observed in the UV–vis absorption spectra (Fig. 3d), further confirming the successful deposition of p-CQDs.

The XRD pattern (Fig. S13a) revealed that the diffraction peaks of WS2 NSs were consistent with those of previously reported [29]. After p-CQD deposition, a new broad diffraction peak located at approximately 26° appeared in the XRD pattern of p-CQD/WS2 hybrids (Fig. S13b), which can be ascribed to the characteristic peak of p-CQDs. We then utilized the XPS spectra to investigate the chemical structure of p-CQD/WS2 hybrids. As depicted in Fig. 3e, five predominant peaks ascribed to W 4f, S 2p, C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s can be detected in the survey XPS spectra of p-CQD/WS2 hybrids. In the high-resolution W 4f spectrum, three typical peaks at 32.0, 34.2, and 36.5 eV were ascribed to W 4f7/2, W 4f5/2, W 5p3/2, respectively. Furthermore, the presence of S2− can be verified by the high-resolution S 2p spectrum, which revealed S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 peaks at 161.7 and 162.8 eV, respectively. Additionally, the C–S, CC, C–N, C–OH, –NH2, graphitic N, O–H, and C–O were observed in the high-resolution C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s spectra, respectively, which were consistent with those of pristine p-CQDs (Fig. 1g).

Having investigated the structure characteristic of p-CQD/WS2 hybrids, we then evaluated the antibacterial activity of p-CQD/WS2 in vitro and in vivo. A standard colony counting method was used to evaluate the in vitro antibacterial activity of p-CQD/WS2 in two non-MDR bacterial strains (E. coli and S. aureus) and one strain of MDR bacteria (MRSA). As shown in Fig. S14, p-CQD/WS2 hybrids exhibited excellent inhibitory activities against the three bacterial strains with an increased concentration from 0 to 100 μg/mL. We then evaluated the in vivo antibacterial efficacy of p-CQD/WS2 hybrids using the MRSA-infected skin wounds in mice as a model. Fig. S15a revealed the wound photographs of mice in the control group and p-CQD/WS2 group at 0, 2, 4, 6, 9, and 12 days. Compared with the control groups, obvious reduction of wounds area was detected in the p-CQD/WS2 group after 4 days. Similar to the pristine p-CQDs, the wound area decreased below 5% in the p-CQD/WS2 group after 12 days, indicating the excellent antibacterial activity of p-CQD/WS2 hybrids. Moreover, the number of CFU in the p-CQD/WS2 group at 12 days were much less than those in the control group (Fig. S15b), further confirming their antibacterial property against multidrug-resistant bacteria in vivo. These results demonstrated that the great antibacterial property of p-CQDs will not disappear after depositing on the surface of WS2 NSs, which could be ascribed to the controlled release of p-CQDs from WS2 NSs.

The controlled release behaviors of p-CQDs were then assessed by the fluorescence spectra. Fig. S16a revealed that p-CQDs exhibited a strong emission peak at 430 nm, while p-CQD/WS2 hybrids (100 μg/mL) showed a significant fluorescence quenching compared with the pristine p-CQDs (100 μg/mL). The electronic structure of p-CQD/WS2 hybrids were then investigated to understand their fluorescence quenching mechanism. As depicted in Fig. S16b, the electron on the conduction band (CB) of p-CQDs could be transferred to that of WS2 NSs, resulting in significant fluorescence quenching. Fig. S16c revealed that the quenched fluorescence of p-CQD/WS2 hybrids was recovery after incubation for different times, suggesting the controlled release of p-CQDs from WS2 NSs. Owing to the excellent antibacterial activity of p-CQDs with low MIC values (3 μg/mL), the released p-CQDs from p-CQD/WS2 hybrids (100 μg/mL) were enough to kill bacteria. Therefore, the strong electrostatic and hydrophobic interactions between the released p-CQDs and bacterial membrane could induce bacterial membrane damage.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro antibacterial activity and osteoinductivity of p-CQD/WS2/n-CQD/GelMA hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: We next fabricated the p-CQD/WS2 and n-CQD co-incorporated GelMA hydrogels through a one-step photopolymerization in the presence of photoinitiator (Fig. 3a). After p-CQD/WS2 and n-CQD incorporation, the obvious color change of GelMA hydrogels was observed in the p-CQD/WS2/n-CQD/GelMA hydrogels (Fig. 4 a), which was convenient for us to confirm the successful loading and detect the dynamic release of p-CQD/WS2 hybrids and n-CQDs. We then performed the SEM characterization to observe the surface morphology of these hydrogels. Fig. 4b showed the representative porous sponge-like structure of these GelMA hydrogels, indicating that the loading of p-CQD/WS2 hybrids and n-CQDs exhibited insignificant effect on the excellent water absorption capacity of pristine hydrogels. No statistical difference of the pore size was observed in the GelMA hydrogel group and p-CQD/WS2/n-CQD/GelMA hydrogel group, but the pore walls of the hydrogel membrane became significantly rough after the addition of p-CQD/WS2 and n-CQDs, which was more favorable for cell adhesion due to a larger specific surface area [58]. The significantly rough pore walls also indicated the successful loading of p-CQD/WS2 hybrids and n-CQDs. The swelling rate of the pristine GelMA hydrogel and p-CQD/WS2/n-CQD/GelMA hydrogel exhibited insignificant change (Fig. 4c), suggesting that the introduction of p-CQD/WS2 and n-CQDs revealed no effect on the water absorption capacity of GelMA hydrogel. We then evaluated the degradation rate of these hydrogel scaffolds, which was crucial to the bone regeneration. As presented in Fig. S17, the remaining mass of p-CQD/WS2/n-CQD/GelMA hydrogels decreased to approximately 40% after immersing in DI water, PBS, or DMEM, which was similar to that of pristine GelMA hydrogels, demonstrating that the suitable degradation rate of GelMA hydrogels would not change after encapsulating p-CQD/WS2 hybrids and n-CQDs. The mechanical properties of the four GelMA hydrogel scaffold were assessed using an Instron 5944 testing system. Compared to the pristine GelMA hydrogel group, a slight increase of the compression modulus was observed in the p-CQD/WS2/n-CQD/GelMA hydrogel group (Fig. 4d), demonstrating that the mechanical properties of GelMA hydrogel scaffolds were improved by incorporating p-CQD/WS2 hybrids and n-CQDs.

Having confirmed the excellent water absorption ability, outstanding mechanical properties, and suitable degradation rate of p-CQD/WS2/n-CQD/GelMA hydrogels, we then investigated whether the excellent antibacterial activity of p-CQDs was retained after being encapsulated in hydrogels. Fig. 4e showed that obvious colony on the LB agar plates can be observed in the control and n-CQD/GelMA hydrogel group, demonstrating that n-CQDs with negative charge have no antibacterial activity. In contrast, no colony of E. coli, S. aureus, and MRSA on the LB agar plates were observed in the p-CQD/WS2 hybrid encapsulated GelMA hydrogel group after incubating for 24 h, verifying their outstanding antibacterial activity. Apart from the standard dilution method, we further utilized live/dead staining assay to evaluate the antibacterial activity of p-CQD/WS2/n-CQD/GelMA hydrogels. As displayed in Fig. S18, the excellent antibacterial ability of p-CQD/WS2/n-CQD/GelMA hydrogels can be confirmed by the fluorescent images, illustrating that obvious red fluorescent was detected in MRSA. These results forcefully demonstrated that p-CQD/WS2 incorporated GelMA hydrogels possessed excellent antibacterial activity against non-MDR and MDR bacterial strains, which was similar to the pristine p-CQD/WS2 hybrids.

Prior to evaluate the osteogenic differentiation activity, the in vitro biocompatibility of p-CQDs, p-CQD/WS2, n-CQDs and their hydrogel formulation against hMSCs were first assessed. The excellent biocompatibility of p-CQDs, p-CQD/WS2 hybrids, and n-CQDs was preliminarily demonstrated by the MTT assay, exhibiting no obvious cytotoxicity of these samples even at the concentration of 500 μg/mL (Fig. S19b-d). Apart from MTT assay, we further investigated the ROS levels in the hMSCs treated with p-CQDs, p-CQD/WS2 hybrids, or n-CQDs by employing dihydrodichlorofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA) as the fluorescent probe and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) as the positive control. After incubation with p-CQDs, p-CQD/WS2 hybrids, or n-CQDs for 24 h, no significant increase in the ROS level can be detected (Fig. S20), implying that no oxidative stress of the hMSCs was triggered by p-CQDs, p-CQD/WS2 hybrids, or n-CQDs. Furthermore, no significant cytotoxicity of GelMA, n-CQD/GelMA, p-CQD/WS2/GelMA, and p-CQD/WS2/n-CQD/GelMA hydrogel against hMSCs can be detected (Fig. S19a). For live/dead staining assay, the obvious green fluorescent was observed in the four hydrogel-treated hMSCs after incubating for 7 or 14 days (Fig. S21), suggesting the outstanding biocompatibility of p-CQD/WS2/n-CQD/GelMA hydrogels. We then performed the alkaline phosphatase (ALP) activity and ARS staining to assess the osteogenic activity of these hydrogels. As depicted in Fig. 5 b, insignificantly enhanced ALP activity of hMSCs in pristine GelMA group was observed, whereas the n-CQD/GelMA group and p-CQD/WS2/GelMA group exhibited an enhanced ALP activity after 7 days of treatment. Notably, the ALP activity of hMSCs in p-CQD/WS2/n-CQD/GelMA hydrogel group was dramatically increased by 2.35 folds, suggesting that the higher osteoinductivity was achieved by incorporating n-CQDs and p-CQD/WS2 hybrids. Besides, compared to the pristine GelMA hydrogels, the hMSC cultured on the n-CQD/GelMA hydrogels and p-CQD/WS2/GelMA hydrogels for 14 days exhibited an obvious more nodule (Fig. 5a). Significantly, a dramatically more nodule of the hMSC in the p-CQD/WS2/n-CQD/GelMA hydrogel group can be observed than others, indicating that the n-CQD and p-CQD/WS2 co-incorporated GelMA hydrogels possessed the highest osteogenic potential. The quantitative ARS assay displayed in Fig. 5c further demonstrated that the incorporation of negatively charged n-CQDs could promote the osteogenic differentiation of hMSCs.

Having confirmed the excellent antibacterial activity and osteoinductivity in vitro, we then evaluated the positive effect of p-CQD/WS2 and n-CQD co-modified hydrogel on repair of infected bone defects. After anesthesia, the mice were operated to create an infected craniotomy defect model (diameter = 6 mm) with the addition of 100 μL MRSA (1 × 108 CFU/mL). Then, the GelMA, n-CQDs, p-CQD/WS2, and photoinitiator were injecting into the defect site, followed by the 1 min irradiation using UV light. After the 0, 30, or 60-day implantation, we utilized the Micro-CT to evaluate the osteogenesis efficiency of p-CQD/WS2/n-CQD/GelMA hydrogels. As shown in Fig. 5e, almost no newly bone was observed in the GelMA hydrogel group and p-CQD/WS2/GelMA group even at 60 days. Besides, the defect regions had been partly filled in the n-CQD-modified GelMA hydrogel group at 30 or 60 days, indicating that the n-CQD/GelMA hydrogels without antibacterial ability cannot completely repair infected bone defect. In contrast, the new bone area in the p-CQD/WS2/n-CQD/GelMA hydrogel group (45.1 ± 2.2%) at 30 days was much higher than that in the n-CQD-modified GelMA hydrogel group (12.1 ± 1.5%), further confirming the accelerated bone regeneration owing to the excellent antibacterial activity of p-CQD/WS2. Moreover, the bone defects were nearly completely healed in the p-CQD/WS2/n-CQD/GelMA hydrogel group with the new bone area of 97.0 ± 1.6% at 60 days (Fig. 5d), which was much higher than that of n-CQD/GelMA hydrogel group (36.1 ± 2.0%). The enhanced osteogenesis efficiency of p-CQD/WS2/n-CQD/GelMA hydrogels was further investigated by histological analysis (Fig. S22), revealing that a great deal of vessels and medullary cavities formed in the bone defect sites. These results illustrated that the accelerated bone regeneration was achieved in the GelMA hydrogels by incorporating p-CQD/WS2 with antibacterial ability and n-CQDs with osteoinductivity simultaneously. Finally, we evaluated the in vivo biocompatibility of these hydrogel scaffolds using H&E staining. As shown in Fig. S23, no significant long-term toxicity of main organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney) in each groups can be detected during the treatment period of 60 days, illustrating the excellent biosafety of these hydrogel scaffolds.

In summary, the biocompatible CQDs with antibacterial and osteogenic activity were successfully synthesized by rational regulating their surface charge and avoiding aggregation of the positively and negatively charged components. By further fabricating p-CQD/WS2 hybrids and constructing p-CQD/WS2 and n-CQD co-encapsulated GelMA hydrogels, the obtained p-CQD/WS2/n-CQD/GelMA hydrogel scaffolds can not only efficiently kill MDR bacteria but also significantly promote bone regeneration. Compared to toxic antibiotics and metal ions, a series of in vitro and in vivo tests demonstrated the required biocompatibility of the antibacterial p-CQDs, without showing any negative effect on osteogenesis differentiation. In a contaminated craniotomy defect model, the multifunctional p-CQD/WS2/n-CQD/GelMA hydrogel scaffolds achieved nearly complete repair of infected bone defect at 60 days after implanting. Owing to the sustained release behaviors and injectable features, the antibacterial and osteogenic CQD co-incorporated GelMA hydrogel scaffolds could be a promising candidate for treating infected bone defects.

Bijiang Geng: Investigation, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Ping Li: Investigation, Methodology. Fuling Fang: Visualization, Formal analysis. Wenyan Shi: Investigation, Methodology. Julie Glowacki: Visualization, Writing – review & editing. Dengyu Pan: Conceptualization, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.
